title: The Effect of Corporate Taxes on Investment and Entrepreneurship

revisited_by.title: The value added of machine learning to causal inference: evidence from revisited studies

identification.narrative: Djankov et al. (2010) estimate the effect of corporate tax rates on investment and entrepreneurship using cross-country OLS regressions for the year 2004. Three measures of corporate taxes (statutory rate, first-year effective rate, five-year effective rate) are regressed on four outcome variables (investment/GDP, FDI/GDP, business density, entry rate) with up to 12 control variables. The key finding is that higher corporate taxes reduce investment and entrepreneurship, but estimates become insignificant when all controls are included simultaneously. Baiardi & Naghi (2024) revisit the 'kitchen sink' specification (Table 5D) using DML, finding larger and often significant effects because DML handles the high-dimensional controls more efficiently.

main_results.spec: DML (Lasso) — Investment ~ five-year effective rate, all 12 controls
main_results.notes: Baiardi & Naghi (2024) DML estimate; original OLS = -0.189 (SE 0.118)

main_results.spec: OLS baseline — Investment ~ five-year effective rate, all 12 controls
main_results.notes: Original Djankov et al. (2010) Table 5D estimate

main_results.spec: DML (Lasso) — FDI ~ five-year effective rate, all 12 controls
main_results.notes: Baiardi & Naghi (2024) DML estimate; original OLS = -0.095 (SE 0.081)

main_results.spec: OLS baseline — FDI ~ five-year effective rate, all 12 controls
main_results.notes: Original Djankov et al. (2010) Table 5D estimate

main_results.spec: DML (Lasso) — Business density ~ five-year effective rate, all 12 controls
main_results.notes: Baiardi & Naghi (2024) DML estimate; original OLS = -0.070 (SE 0.103)

main_results.spec: DML (Lasso) — Entry rate ~ five-year effective rate, all 12 controls
main_results.notes: Baiardi & Naghi (2024) DML estimate; original OLS = -0.133 (SE 0.103). DML estimate is significant at 5%.

main_results.spec: DML (Boosting) — Investment ~ five-year effective rate, all 12 controls
main_results.notes: Baiardi & Naghi (2024) DML Boosting estimate — significant at 5%

main_results.spec: DML (Forest) — Investment ~ five-year effective rate, all 12 controls
main_results.notes: Baiardi & Naghi (2024) DML Random Forest estimate — significant at 5%

dml.rationale: PLR (Partial Linear Regression) is appropriate because the original study uses OLS with no instruments — the treatment (corporate tax rate) is continuous and directly observed. The DML-PLR framework allows flexible ML-based estimation of nuisance functions (outcome ~ controls and treatment ~ controls) while preserving valid inference on the linear treatment effect, exactly as in Baiardi & Naghi (2024).
